title: Glass and Mirror Installation Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Glass and Mirror Installation Business: Real Costs and Earnings
metaDescription: Honest guide to starting a glass and mirror installation business: real startup costs, glazier earnings, licensing, finding clients, and what most people get wrong.
bestFor: Hands-on people who can cut, lift, and set glass precisely and want a trade with strong margins on custom and commercial work
biggestRisk: A single mismeasured or broken custom panel can wipe out a job's profit, and serious injury from glass handling is a constant hazard
whatItIs:
A glass and mirror installation business measures, fabricates, and installs glass products for homes and businesses. The bread-and-butter work is frameless shower enclosures, wall and gym mirrors, glass railings, table tops, and replacement window and storefront glass. Residential glaziers lean toward shower doors and mirrors; commercial glaziers do storefronts, office partitions, and curtain wall. Most small operators start residential because the jobs are smaller, the equipment is cheaper, and a homeowner shower-door job can be quoted and installed within a week.

Glass is unforgiving. It cuts skin, shatters when mishandled, and tempered or laminated panels often have to be ordered to exact size with no second chance to trim them on site. That difficulty is also why margins are healthy: most people will not touch it, supply is consolidated, and a clean frameless shower install routinely sells for $1,000 to $3,500. The work rewards precise measuring, careful handling, and a relationship with a reliable glass supplier or fabricator who cuts and tempers to your specs.
dailyReality: A typical day is a mix of measuring, fabricating or picking up cut glass, and installing on site. You might template a shower in the morning, drive to a supplier to collect a tempered order, and hang a wall of mirrors in the afternoon. Expect heavy, awkward lifting, frameless shower glass and large mirrors are deceptively heavy and require suction cups, a helper, and patience. Around the install work there is steady time spent measuring twice, quoting, ordering glass to exact dimensions, and coordinating with general contractors whose schedules slip. Cuts, slivers, and the occasional cracked panel are part of the job; good operators slow down to stay safe and accurate.

startupCostsItemized.item: Hand tools (suction cups, glass cutters, setting blocks, levels, drills)
Measuring and templating tools (laser measure, story poles, digital templating)
Work vehicle / van with glass rack or A-frame
Business registration / LLC + contractor license where required
Initial glass and hardware inventory (mirror, channel, shower hardware)
Shop space for fabrication and storage
Google Business Profile, simple website, vehicle signage

realEarnings.yearOne: Beginners with real glazing skill but a new business commonly earn $3,000 to $6,000 per month while building a referral base and learning to price accurately. Operators who came out of a glass shop and bring contractor relationships can hit $6,000 to $9,000 sooner. Expect a slow start as you build trust with contractors and homeowners.
realEarnings.experienced: Established residential glaziers with steady contractor relationships and good reviews commonly report $8,000 to $16,000 per month solo or with one helper, driven heavily by frameless shower work, which carries the best margins. Adding small commercial glass repair and storefront work smooths out seasonality.
realEarnings.topEarners: Shops with a crew, a fabrication space, and standing commercial accounts (property managers, general contractors, retail chains) gross $40,000 to $150,000+ per month, but that requires employees, a glass inventory, equipment, and a real estimator. Reaching it means becoming a shop owner and bidder, not an installer, and most never make that jump.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Mix of work matters most: frameless showers and custom mirror jobs pay far better per hour than basic window-glass replacement. Accurate measuring (so you do not eat the cost of a re-ordered tempered panel) and a reliable, fairly priced supplier are the next biggest levers.
realEarnings.perHour: Effective rates for skilled solo glaziers commonly run $60 to $130 per hour of install time on custom work, lower on commodity window replacement. Counting measuring, supplier runs, quoting, and the occasional broken panel, realistic blended rates are often $45 to $90 per hour.

howToStart: Before you start: Get real glazing experience first, ideally a year or more in a glass shop or as a glazier's helper. This is not a trade to learn on a customer's $2,000 shower. Confirm whether your state or city requires a contractor or specialty glazing license; rules vary widely.

Weeks 1-2: Register the business, get general liability insurance, and line up a glass supplier or fabricator who will cut, edge, and temper to your specs. Buy core hand tools, suction cups, and a laser measure. Set up a vehicle that can transport glass safely.

Weeks 3-6: Quote and complete your first jobs, start with simpler mirror and table-top work, then frameless showers as your measuring gets dialed in. Photograph every finished install and ask for reviews. Introduce yourself to local general contractors, remodelers, and bathroom remodelers who need a reliable glazier.

Months 2-4: Build a standard price sheet for common jobs (shower enclosures, mirror walls, glass railings) so quoting is fast and profitable. Start pursuing small commercial repair and storefront work for steadier volume.

Months 4-12: Decide whether to add a helper and a small fabrication/storage space. Lock in two or three contractor relationships that feed you repeat work, that referral pipeline is worth more than any single big job.

mustHave: Real glass-handling and installation skill, cutting, edging, setting, and sealing without breakage or injury
Precise measuring and templating, because tempered and laminated glass cannot be trimmed on site
Physical strength and care for heavy, awkward, dangerous lifting
Comfort coordinating with contractors and quoting custom work
canLearn: Frameless shower hardware systems and the quirks of each manufacturer
Digital templating for complex or out-of-square openings
Reading commercial glazing specs and bidding small storefront jobs
separators: Measuring accuracy that virtually eliminates re-ordered panels, the single biggest profit killer in this trade
Relationships with remodelers and general contractors who feed you steady, well-paying custom work
Knowing which jobs to price high or decline (commodity window glass) versus where the real margin is (frameless showers, custom mirror, railings)

whatPeopleGetWrong: Trying to learn the trade on paying customers, glass mistakes are expensive, dangerous, and visible, and bad early installs destroy your reputation
Mismeasuring a tempered or laminated panel, then eating the cost and the delay of re-ordering it
Underpricing frameless shower work, which is skilled, risky, and where the real margin lives
Skimping on safety, gloves, suction handling, and a helper for big panels, and ending up with serious cuts or a shattered job
Not nailing down a reliable supplier, leaving you stuck when glass arrives late, wrong, or damaged
Ignoring licensing rules and getting flagged on a permitted job or insurance claim

toolsAndEquipment.item: Glass suction cups and lifting handles
toolsAndEquipment.note: Essential for safe handling of mirrors and shower glass. Buy quality; a dropped panel is far more expensive.
Accurate measurement is the whole game. A good laser measure pays for itself the first time it prevents a re-order.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Glass cutters, grinders, and edging tools
toolsAndEquipment.note: For on-site fitting of mirror and annealed glass. Tempered glass cannot be cut and must be ordered to size.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Drills, hole saws, and diamond bits
toolsAndEquipment.note: For mounting hardware and drilling glass without cracking it.
Transports panels upright and secure. Retrofit a van or use a trailer.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Cut-resistant gloves, eye protection, knee pads
toolsAndEquipment.note: Non-negotiable. Glass injuries are common and serious.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Silicone guns, setting blocks, shims, sealants
toolsAndEquipment.note: Consumables for every install. Keep them stocked.

methods: Relationships with general contractors, remodelers, and bathroom/kitchen renovators who need a dependable glazier on call
A Google Business Profile with sharp before/after photos of frameless showers and mirror work, plus steady reviews
Referrals from interior designers, home builders, and real estate agents staging or finishing homes
Local Facebook groups and Nextdoor for residential shower-door and mirror leads
Direct outreach to property managers and small retailers for storefront and commercial glass repair
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Homeowners doing bathroom remodels (shower enclosures), people finishing gyms and closets (mirrors), and contractors who need a glass sub. Commercial customers are property managers, retailers, and general contractors needing storefront and partition glass.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: First jobs usually come within three to eight weeks through local listings and direct contractor outreach. A steady pipeline fed by two or three repeat contractor relationships typically takes six to twelve months to build.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Broad paid advertising and a polished brand before you have photos and reviews. Early on, contractor relationships and a portfolio of clean installs convert far better than any ad spend.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes, and faster than many trades because custom glass is well-paid and undersupplied. A skilled solo glazier with contractor relationships can reach a full-time income within the first year. The solo ceiling is set by how many installs your body and your truck can handle in a week.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Possible but it requires becoming a shop. Hiring installers and an estimator lets you take more and bigger jobs, but you take on payroll, glass inventory, breakage risk, and the need to bid accurately. Stepping back fully means trusting crews with measuring, where a single error is costly.
howItScales.sellable: Established glass shops with commercial accounts, a fabrication space, equipment, and trained staff do sell, often for a meaningful multiple of profit because the supplier relationships and recurring contracts have real value. A pure solo install operation is harder to sell because it is mostly your hands and reputation.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: A fabrication/storage shop, glass inventory and supplier credit, trained installers, an accurate estimating system for commercial bids, and standing relationships with general contractors. The jump from solo installer to shop owner is the hard part.

strongFit: You already have glazing experience or are committed to learning it properly before taking paid work
You are physically strong, careful, and comfortable around a material that can cut and shatter
You enjoy precise, finish-quality work and measuring things exactly right
You can build relationships with contractors and quote custom jobs confidently
poorFit: You want a low-skill, low-risk business you can start this weekend with no training
You are careless with measurements or rush through detail work
You cannot do heavy, awkward lifting or do not want a helper for big panels
You are not willing to carry proper insurance and safety gear
askYourself: Do I have enough real glass experience to install a $2,500 frameless shower without learning on the customer?
Can I reliably measure to a sixteenth of an inch and order tempered glass with no margin for error?
Are there contractors and remodelers in my area who need a glazier, and a supplier nearby who will cut and temper to my specs?

faqs.question: Do I need a license to install glass?
faqs.answer: It depends heavily on your state and the type of work. Many states require a contractor's license (sometimes a specialty glazing classification) for installation work above a certain dollar amount, especially commercial. Residential mirror and small jobs are often less regulated. Always confirm your local rules and carry general liability insurance before any paid work.

faqs.question: Can I cut tempered glass to size on the job?
faqs.answer: No. Tempered (safety) glass cannot be cut or drilled after tempering, it shatters into fragments. It must be ordered to exact size from a fabricator, which is why precise measuring matters so much. You can cut annealed glass and most mirror on site, but shower and railing glass is almost always tempered and ordered to spec.

faqs.question: How much can I charge for a frameless shower install?
faqs.answer: Frameless shower enclosures commonly sell for $1,000 to $3,500 installed, depending on glass thickness, hardware, and complexity. They are the best-margin residential job in this trade because they require skill and precise measuring that most people will not attempt. Custom and curved enclosures go higher.

faqs.question: Is glass installation dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is one of the real downsides. Cuts, slivers, and the risk of a heavy panel shattering or being dropped are constant. Cut-resistant gloves, suction handling, eye protection, and a helper for large panels are essential. Experienced glaziers slow down and respect the material; rushing is how people get hurt.

faqs.question: Should I start with residential or commercial glass work?
faqs.answer: Most solo operators start residential, shower doors, mirrors, table tops, and railings, because the jobs are smaller, the equipment is cheaper, and you control the schedule. Commercial storefront and curtain wall work pays well and is steadier but usually requires a license, more equipment, crews, and the ability to bid larger jobs.

faqs.question: How important is the glass supplier relationship?
faqs.answer: Critical. A reliable fabricator who cuts, edges, and tempers accurately and on time is the backbone of the business. Late, wrong, or damaged glass costs you scheduled jobs and customer trust. Many successful glaziers build their business around one or two suppliers they trust completely.

faqs.question: Can I run this part-time around a job?
faqs.answer: It is difficult. Glass work involves coordinating supplier orders, contractor schedules, and on-site installs that rarely fit neatly into evenings and weekends, plus heavy lifting that benefits from a helper. Some operators start with weekend mirror and table-top work, but the well-paying shower and commercial jobs generally require weekday availability.

dataSources: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Glaziers (occupational wages and outlook)
National Glass Association / glazing industry trade reports (market and pricing trends)
Angi / HomeAdvisor, Shower Door and Mirror Installation Cost Guides (reported job pricing ranges)
Operator discussions in glazing and contractor communities (real-world pricing, measuring, and supplier practices)
